title: Etomidate
body_html:
Etomidate is a short-acting intravenous anaesthetic agent used for the induction of general anaesthesia and sedation. It is classed as a non-barbiturate hypnotic.

Mechanism of action
Etomidate binds to GABAA receptors and prolongs the post-synaptic inhibitory effect of GABA in the thalamus.

It is metabolised in the liver. The half-life of etomidate is around 75 minutes but recovery from etomidate is rapid with little residual depression as a result of extensive redistribution and rapid metabolism. At the typical dose, anaesthesia is induced for approximately 5–10 minutes with onset 30-60 seconds after administration.

Side effects
Common side effects:
  • Apnoea
  • Hypotension
  • Movement disorders (extraneous muscle movements can be minimised by an opioid analgesic or a short-acting benzodiazepine given just before induction)
  • Nausea & vomiting
  • Respiratory disorders
  • Skin reactions
  • Vascular pain (injection pain can be reduced by giving an opioid analgesic just before induction)

Cautions and contraindications
  • Acute circulatory failure (shock)
  • Adrenal insufficiency
  • Acute porphyrias
  • Cardiovascular disease
  • Elderly
  • Fixed cardiac output
  • Hypovolaemia

Adrenal insufficiency
Etomidate suppresses adrenocortical function, particularly during continuous administration, and it should not be used for maintenance of anaesthesia. It should be used with caution in patients with underlying adrenal insufficiency e.g. patients with sepsis.

Comparison to other induction agents

Etomidate is associated with rapid recovery without a hangover effect. Etomidate causes less hypotension than thiopental sodium and propofol during induction. It produces a high incidence of extraneous muscle movements, which can be minimised by an opioid analgesic or a short-acting benzodiazepine given just before induction.

There are four commonly used induction agents in the UK. These are summarised in the table below:

DrugKey PointsDoseSide effects
Propofol 1%
  • Produces significant venodilation
  • Myocardial depression
  • Can reduce cerebral perfusion pressure
1.5-2.5 mg/kgHypotension
Respiratory depression
Pain at site of injection
Ketamine
  • Produces dissociative state
  • Does not display a dose-response continuum
  • Causes bronchodilation (useful in asthma)
Initially 0.5-2 mg/kg

Typical IV dose is 1.5mg/kg		Tachycardia
Hypertension
Laryngospasm
Unpleasant hallucinations
Nausea and vomiting
Hypersalivation
Increased intracranial and intraocular pressure
Nystagmus and diplopia
Abnormal movements
Skin reactions
Thiopentone
(thiopental sodium,
sodium pentothal)
  • Ultra-short acting barbiturate
  • Acts on the GABA receptor complex
  • Decreases cerebral metabolic oxygen
  • Reduces cerebral blood flow & ICP
Adult: 3-5 mg/kg
Child: 5-8 mg/kg
Doses halved in hypovolaemia		Venodilation
Myocardial depression
Hypotension

Contraindicated in:
Acute porphyrias
Myotonic dystrophy
Etomidate
  • Most haemodynamically stable induction agent
  • Useful in hypovolemia, anaphylaxis & asthma
  • Similar cerebral effects to thiopentone
0.15-0.3 mg/kgInjection site pain
Movement disorders
Adrenal insufficiency
Apnoea

Contraindicated in:
Sepsis (due to adrenal suppression)
